title: GUIDELINES FOR THE DEVELOPMENT OF EQUIVALENCE AGREEMENTS REGARDING FOOD IMPORT AND EXPORT INSPECTION AND CERTIFICATION SYSTEMS

sections.section: GUIDELINES FOR THE DEVELOPMENT OF EQUIVALENCE AGREEMENTS REGARDING FOOD IMPORT AND EXPORT INSPECTION AND CERTIFICATION SYSTEMS
sections.summary: Document title heading only identifying the guidelines for development of equivalence agreements on food import and export inspection and certification systems (no substantive provisions in this heading).
Document code heading only (CAC/GL 34-1999) with no substantive content in this item.

Provides practical guidance for governments seeking bilateral or multilateral equivalence agreements on food import/export inspection and certification systems, noting such agreements may be binding international agreements under the Vienna Convention or less formal arrangements like memoranda of understanding.

Defines key terms used in the guidelines—audit, certification, certification/inspection systems, equivalence, inspection, official vs. officially recognized systems, and requirements—and references relevant Codex principles (e.g., CAC/GL 20-1995; CAC/GL 26-1997).

Describes the purposes of agreements on food import/export inspection and certification—assuring exported products meet importing-country requirements, reducing duplication and conserving resources, and enabling cooperative exchange of expertise and information; states equivalence agreements are generally not intended as a trade precondition but to meet import requirements with minimal impediments (e.g., reduced physical checks or additional certification) and notes that 'country' may include regional economic integration organizations and that competent authorities may enter such arrangements.

sections.section: SECTION 4 - SCOPE AND TYPES OF AGREEMENTS
sections.summary: States the guidelines cover bilateral and multilateral agreements (one- or two-way trade) which may address any aspect of food safety or other relevant requirements, be limited to specific products or areas, apply where equivalence is established for some or all requirements, and may include provisions for certificates or for dispensing with certificates.

sections.section: SECTION 5 - CONSIDERATIONS BEFORE ENTERING INTO BILATERAL OR MULTILATERAL DISCUSSIONS
sections.summary: Requires the importing country to determine objectively whether exporting measures meet its requirements and advises countries to prioritize negotiations because significant resources are needed—priorities may consider public health risk, trade volume, exporting-country infrastructure and compliance history, adherence to the Codex Code of Ethics, and potential resource savings; parties should facilitate pre- and post-agreement assessments, and countries not ready may build capacity through information exchange, joint training, technical cooperation, and possible technical assistance from developed importing countries.

sections.section: SECTION 6 - INITIATING DISCUSSIONS TOWARD AN EQUIVALENCE AGREEMENT
sections.summary: Requires the initiating country to identify the proposed agreement type, covered products, competent authority(ies), and scope of requirements; obliges the approached country to respond promptly or provide reasons and recommendations if it cannot proceed; and requires both parties to verify legal authority to negotiate and conclude the agreement.

sections.section: SECTION 7 - CONSULTATIVE PROCESS FOR EQUIVALENCE AGREEMENTS
sections.summary: Outlines the consultative process: the importing country should provide its control measure texts and objectives (including health risks) and the exporting country should demonstrate its control system achieves the importing country's objectives or level of protection, with Codex use encouraged and a detailed exchange of legal, operational, laboratory, inspection, audit and rapid-alert information recommended to support assessment and verification and ongoing audits and problem resolution; it also requires agreeing procedures for lists of approved establishments (as an alternative to certificates), emergency information exchange, handling non-compliant shipments, termination of agreements, and provision for public comment while respecting confidentiality.

sections.section: 17. The exporting country should provide information that demonstrates that its own safety  control  system  achieves  the  importing  country's  objectives  and/or  level  of protection, as appropriate:
sections.summary: Specifies that equivalence agreements for food safety (sanitary) measures are concluded only after an importing country determines the exporting country's control measures achieve the importing country's appropriate level of health protection, and that agreements for other requirements are concluded when the exporting measures meet the importing country's objectives.

Allows competent authorities to conduct a trial or pilot study before concluding an agreement and indicates pilot protocols may cover the trial timeframe, roles of government and recognized private organizations, inspection and certification procedures, audit frequency, and training or information needs.

sections.section: 30.  Before  entering  into  an  agreement,  the  competent  authorities  in  the  importing  and  exporting  countries may agree to the conduct of a trial or pilot study.
sections.summary: States that competent authorities of importing and exporting countries may agree to conduct a trial or pilot study before entering into an equivalence agreement.
Indicates that Appendix A lists information that may be included, as appropriate, in an equivalence agreement.
Requires signatory governments to publish a notice announcing the agreement or publish the agreement text, and to make the text available to the public in each country's official language(s).
Appendix A heading only; no substantive content provided in this section.

Lists recommended contents of equivalence agreements, including title, parties, purpose, scope, definitions, substantive obligations, competent authorities, statement of equivalence findings, assessment and verification methods, certification criteria, sampling and analytical methods, administrative procedures, information exchange, transparency and notification procedures, dispute settlement, liaison officials, entry into force, review/modification/termination procedures, and signatures.
